anchor.snippet: the distributive property of binary operations is a generalization of the distributive law
For example, in elementary arithmetic
the operation [MATH] is left-distributive over (or with respect to) [MATH] if, given any elements
the operation [MATH] is right-distributive over [MATH] if, given any elements
and the operation [MATH] is distributive over [MATH] if it is left- and right-distributive
When [MATH] is commutative , the three conditions above are logically equivalent

annotations.label: Left vs right distributivity under commutativity

anchor.snippet: If the operation outside the parentheses (in this case, the multiplication) is commutative, then left-distributivity implies right-distributivity
One example of an operation that is "only" right-distributive is division

annotations.label: Distributive laws in rings and fields

anchor.snippet: The distributive laws are among the axioms for rings
When a sum is multiplied by a sum, multiply each summand of a sum with each summand of the other sum
During mental arithmetic, distributivity is often used unconsciously
Since the factor [MATH] occurs in all summands, it can be factored out
The distributive law is valid for matrix multiplication
Multiplication of ordinal numbers , in contrast, is only left-distributive, not right-distributive

annotations.label: Cross product distributes over vector addition

anchor.snippet: The cross product is left- and right-distributive over vector addition
For sets , the union is distributive over intersection , and intersection is distributive over union
Logical disjunction ("or") is distributive over logical conjunction ("and"), and vice versa
the maximum operation is distributive over the minimum operation, and vice versa
the greatest common divisor is distributive over the least common multiple

annotations.label: Addition distributes over max and min

anchor.snippet: For real numbers, addition distributes over the maximum operation, and also over the minimum operation
For binomial multiplication, distribution is sometimes referred to as the FOIL Method

annotations.label: Ring as semiring with additive inverses

anchor.snippet: A ring is a semiring with additive inverses
A lattice is another kind of algebraic structure with two binary operations

annotations.label: Boolean algebra as ring or distributive lattice

anchor.snippet: A Boolean algebra can be interpreted either as a special kind of ring

annotations.label: Product of sums equals sum of products

anchor.snippet: In any semiring, distributivity can be used to show that any product of sums is a sum of products
